title: The problem with arrays
instruction:
Arrays are great when you know the size upfront. But what if you do not?

You are building a shopping cart. You have no idea how many items the user will add. You cannot write `[5]string` when it might be 2 items or 50.

This is exactly the problem slices solve. Run this and see what happens when you try to add a 4th item to a 3-slot array.

codeChecks.message: Change [3]string to []string to make it a slice instead of an array.

successMessage: Index out of range. The array refused. A slice would have handled that without complaint. That is why slices exist.

title: Declare an empty slice
instruction:
A slice looks almost identical to an array — but with no number in the brackets.

`var names []string` — empty slice, ready to receive values.

An empty slice is not nil. It is just... empty. Length zero, waiting.

Run this and see what an empty slice looks like when printed.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var names []string
	var scores []int
	fmt.Println(names)
	fmt.Println(scores)
	fmt.Println(len(names))
	fmt.Println(len(scores))
}
successMessage: Empty slices print as []. Length is 0. No panic, no error — just ready and waiting.

title: make() — create a slice with a known size
instruction:
When you know how many slots you need upfront, use `make`.

`make([]int, 5)` creates a slice of 5 integers, all set to zero.

This is more efficient than starting empty and appending one by one.

`make([]string, 3)` creates 3 empty string slots.

Run this. Then add a line that creates a `scores` slice of 4 ints using make and prints it.

codeChecks.message: Create a scores slice: scores := make([]int, 4)
Print scores: fmt.Println(scores)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: make creates a slice with a set size, all values at their zero. Use it when you know the size. Use var when you do not.

title: Declare with values
instruction:
When you already have the values, declare and fill in one shot.

`scores := []int{90, 85, 78}`

No size number needed — Go counts the values and sets the length automatically.

Create a `cities` slice with 4 city names and print it.

codeChecks.message: Create a cities slice: cities := []string{"...", ...}
Print it: fmt.Println(cities)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: []type{values} — no size needed. Go counts for you.

title: append — add to the end
instruction:
This is the most important slice operation.

`append(slice, newValue)` returns a new slice with the value added.

Critical rule: you must reassign the result back to the variable.

`names = append(names, "Alex")` ✓
`append(names, "Alex")` ✗ — the original is unchanged

Add three more fruits to the cart using append, one at a time. Print after each.
hint: cart = append(cart, "Eggs")  then fmt.Println(cart). Repeat for two more.

codeChecks.message: Append Eggs: cart = append(cart, "Eggs")
Append Butter: cart = append(cart, "Butter")
Append Juice: cart = append(cart, "Juice")
Replace the TODO lines with your append calls.

successMessage: append returns a new slice — always reassign. That is the one rule beginners forget.

title: len and cap
instruction:
Every slice has two numbers:

- `len(s)` — how many items are currently in the slice
- `cap(s)` — how much space Go has reserved in memory

When you append and the slice runs out of capacity, Go automatically doubles the reserved space. You never manage this yourself — but understanding it explains why cap is sometimes bigger than len.

Run this and watch len and cap change as you append.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	s := []int{}
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(s), cap(s))
	s = append(s, 1)
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(s), cap(s))
	s = append(s, 2)
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(s), cap(s))
	s = append(s, 3)
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(s), cap(s))
	s = append(s, 4)
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(s), cap(s))
	s = append(s, 5)
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(s), cap(s))
}
successMessage: When the slice fills up, Go doubles the capacity. len is what you have. cap is what Go reserved. You use len — Go manages cap.

title: Access and update by index
instruction:
Slices use the same index syntax as arrays. Zero-indexed. Read with `s[i]`. Write with `s[i] = value`.

Change the score at index 2 from its current value to `100`, then print the whole slice.

codeChecks.message: Update index 2: scores[2] = 100
Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: Reading and writing by index works exactly the same as arrays. The flexible part is the size — the access syntax is identical.

title: Loop with range
instruction:
Looping over a slice works exactly like looping over an array.

`for i, v := range scores` gives you the index and value.
`for _, v := range scores` gives you just the value.

Loop through the scores and print each one formatted as `"Score 1: 88"` (use the index + 1 as the number).
hint: for i, v := range scores { fmt.Printf("Score %d: %d\n", i+1, v) }

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for i, v := range scores
Use i+1 so the number starts at 1 not 0.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Same range syntax as arrays. Slices and arrays are nearly identical to work with — slices just do not have a fixed size.

title: Slicing a slice
instruction:
You can cut out a portion of a slice using `[low:high]` syntax.

`scores[1:3]` gives you elements at index 1 and 2 — the high end is exclusive.
`scores[:2]` means from the start up to (not including) index 2.
`scores[2:]` means from index 2 to the end.

Run this. Then add a line that prints just the middle three elements.

codeChecks.message: Print the middle three with scores[1:4] — index 1, 2, and 3.
Replace the TODO comment with your slice expression.

successMessage: Low is inclusive, high is exclusive. scores[1:4] gives you indexes 1, 2, 3. That rule applies everywhere in Go.

title: copy() — the real duplicate
instruction:
Here is a trap that catches almost everyone.

`b := a` does NOT copy a slice. Both `a` and `b` point to the same data. Change one, and you change both.

`copy(dst, src)` creates a genuine separate copy.

Run this and see the trap. Then fix it by replacing `b := a` with a proper copy.
hint: b := make([]int, len(a))  then  copy(b, a)  — two lines replace the one assignment.

codeChecks.message: Create b with make: b := make([]int, len(a))
Copy a into b: copy(b, a)

successMessage: b := a shares the data. copy() separates it. This is one of the most important things to understand about slices in Go.

title: Remove an element
instruction:
Go has no built-in remove function for slices. You build it yourself.

To remove the element at index `i`, stitch the slice back together without it:

`s = append(s[:i], s[i+1:]...)`

The `...` spreads the second slice as individual arguments to append.

Remove the element at index 2 from the names slice and print the result.
hint: names = append(names[:2], names[3:]...)  — this removes index 2.

codeChecks.message: Use append to remove index 2: names = append(names[:2], names[3:]...)
Replace the TODO comment with your remove operation.

successMessage: Slice up to the element, then the rest. Stitch together with append. No built-in delete needed.

title: Slice of slices
instruction:
A slice can hold other slices. Each inner slice can be a different length — unlike a 2D array which must be rectangular.

This is useful for things like a table where rows have different numbers of columns.

Run this. Then add a fourth row with just two subjects.
hint: append the new row: subjects = append(subjects, []string{"Math", "Science"})

codeChecks.message: Append a new row: subjects = append(subjects, []string{"...", "..."})
Replace the TODO comment with your append.

successMessage: Slices of slices are flexible — rows can be different lengths. That is the key difference from a 2D array.

title: Todo list — start
instruction:
Time to build something real.

You are building a todo list app. Start by creating a slice with three tasks and printing the list.
hint: todos := []string{"Buy groceries", "Reply to emails", "Go for a walk"}  then fmt.Println(todos)

codeChecks.message: Create a todos slice: todos := []string{"task1", "task2", "task3"}
Print the list: fmt.Println(todos)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Three tasks. The list exists. Now let's make it do something.

title: Todo list — add more tasks
instruction:
New tasks keep coming in.

Append two more tasks to your todo list. Print the list after each addition so you can see it grow.
hint: todos = append(todos, "Call the dentist")  then fmt.Println(todos). Repeat for a second task.

codeChecks.message: Use append to add a new task: todos = append(todos, "your task")
Add two new tasks using two separate append calls.
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: 5 tasks now. Slices grow as your needs grow. No upfront size required.

title: Todo list — complete a task
instruction:
You finished "Reply to emails" — time to remove it from the list.

It is at index 1. Use the remove pattern to take it out and print the updated list.
expectedOutput: Before: [Buy groceries Reply to emails Go for a walk Call the dentist Read a book]
After:  [Buy groceries Go for a walk Call the dentist Read a book]

codeChecks.message: Remove index 1: todos = append(todos[:1], todos[2:]...)
Replace the TODO comment with your remove.

successMessage: Task done. Removed from the list. One item shorter, just like a real todo app.

title: Todo list — final list
instruction:
One new task just came in: "Plan the weekend".

Append it, then print the complete numbered list:
```
1. Buy groceries
2. Go for a walk
3. Call the dentist
4. Read a book
5. Plan the weekend
```
hint: todos = append(todos, "Plan the weekend")  then loop: for i, t := range todos { fmt.Printf("%d. %s\n", i+1, t) }

codeChecks.message: Append the new task: todos = append(todos, "Plan the weekend")
Use range to loop through todos.
Use i+1 so the numbering starts at 1.
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: A working todo list. You created it, added to it, removed from it, added again, and printed it cleanly. That is a real feature.
